1: Hear this, O house of Jacob, who are called by the name of Israel and who have come forth from the waters of Judah, who swear by the name of YHWH and invoke the God of Israel — but not in truth nor in righteousness. The hearing-call opens the oracle of indictment: Israel's liturgical identity (called by YHWH's name, swearing by YHWH's name) is not matched by truth and righteousness. They perform covenant-identity markers while the substance is absent. In the NT: not everyone who says to me Lord, Lord will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only the one who does the will of my Father (Matthew 7:21). The gap between name-bearing and truth-and-righteousness is the problem Jesus names as the fundamental failure of nominal religion.
2: For they call themselves after the holy city and lean on the God of Israel — YHWH of hosts is his name. The irony is compounded: they name themselves after the holy city and lean on YHWH — but the leaning is not faith; it is presumption. The false security of geographical and ethnic covenant identity is the target. In the NT, John the Baptist addresses the same presumption: Do not think you can say to yourselves, We have Abraham as our father — I tell you that out of these stones God can raise up children for Abraham (Matthew 3:9). The holy-city identity is not transferable to those who do not produce fruit.
3: I declared the former things long ago; they went out from my mouth and I made them known; then suddenly I acted, and they came to pass. YHWH's pattern of announce-then-fulfill is the epistemological basis for trusting the new declarations (vv 6-8): what he foretold happened; therefore what he is now foretelling will happen. The suddenly-I-acted pattern is the NT's pattern of divine intervention — the appointed time of Galatians 4:4 (when the fullness of time had come, God sent his Son) and the sudden-coming of the Son of Man (Matthew 24:27).
4: Because I knew that you are obstinate, and your neck is an iron sinew and your forehead is bronze. The diagnoses of Israel's spiritual condition (obstinate neck, iron sinew, bronze forehead) are the physical metaphors for spiritual hardening: the animal that will not be led, the metal that cannot be bent. In the NT, Stephen cites this tradition before the Sanhedrin: stiff-necked people with uncircumcised hearts and ears! You always resist the Holy Spirit (Acts 7:51). The iron-and-bronze obstinacy is the condition that the Spirit's coming is designed to overcome: I will remove from you your heart of stone and give you a heart of flesh (Ezekiel 36:26).
5: Therefore I told you these things long ago; before they happened I announced them to you, so that you could not say, My idol did them; my image and my metal god ordained them. The pre-announcement strategy is YHWH's argument against idol-attribution: if the events had been announced after the idols were made, the people could have credited the idols. The advance notice removes that option. In the NT, Jesus pre-announces his death and resurrection (Matthew 16:21, Isaiah 17:22-23, Isaiah 20:18-19) so that when it happens the disciples will believe that I am he (John 13:19 — ego eimi, the divine self-identification) — not that the event was coincidence or another power's work.
6: You have heard; look at all of this. Will you not acknowledge it? I am making new things known to you from now on, hidden things you have not known. The transition from the former-things (already announced and fulfilled) to the new things: YHWH now declares hidden things that have not yet been revealed. The new things are the eschatological events that the rest of Second Isaiah announces: the new Exodus, the Servant's mission, the cosmic redemption. In the NT: what no eye has seen, what no ear has heard, what no human mind has conceived — these are the things God has prepared for those who love him, and God has revealed them to us through his Spirit (1 Corinthians 2:9-10). The hidden-things-now-revealed pattern is the NT's understanding of the apostolic gospel.
7: They are created now, not long ago; before today you have not heard of them, so that you could not say, I already knew them. The new things are genuinely new: not the recovery of old information but the novel act of YHWH in the eschatological age. The you-did-not-know defense prevents the claim of prior knowledge. In the NT, the resurrection of the Messiah was not knowable before it happened — even the disciples did not understand that he must rise from the dead (John 20:9). The new thing that YHWH creates is always beyond what the community could have predicted from its existing categories.
8: Yes, you have not heard, you have not known, and from of old your ear has not been opened; for I knew that you were completely treacherous, and that you were called a rebel from the womb. The radical diagnosis: Israel was treacherous from the womb — the same framing as Jacob-the-supplanter (Genesis 25:26), the rebel who later became Israel. The from-the-womb framing anticipates the Servant's calling from the womb (Isaiah 49:1, 5) as the contrast: where Jacob was a rebel from the womb, the Servant is consecrated from the womb. In the NT, Paul applies from-the-womb election to his own calling (Galatians 1:15: from my mother's womb God set me apart) — but the supreme example is the Son himself (Hebrews 10:5-7: when Christ came into the world, he said... I have come to do your will, my God).
9: For the sake of my name I delay my anger; for the sake of my praise I restrain it for you, so that I do not cut you off. YHWH's restraint of deserved judgment is grounded in his own name-reputation, not in Israel's merit — the same as Isaiah 43:25 (I blot out your sins for my own sake). The delay of wrath for the sake of YHWH's name is the theological foundation of the NT's patience: the Lord is not slow in keeping his promise, as some understand slowness; instead he is patient with you, not wanting anyone to perish, but everyone to come to repentance (2 Peter 3:9). The restraint of anger is the expression of the same long-suffering love that drives the Servant's mission.
10: See, I have refined you, but not as silver; I have tested you in the furnace of affliction. The refinement is real — the exile was the furnace of affliction — but the qualification not as silver signals that the refining is not the standard metallurgical process of eliminating impurities through heat alone. YHWH refines through suffering for the purpose of relationship, not merely purification. In the NT: the testing of your faith — of greater worth than gold, which perishes even though refined by fire — results in praise, glory, and honor when Jesus Christ is revealed (1 Peter 1:6-7). The furnace-of-affliction is the NT church's school of faith.
11: For my own sake, for my own sake I act — for how could my name be profaned? And my glory I will not give to another. The double for-my-own-sake (twice, emphatic) grounds every act of YHWH — judgment, refinement, salvation — in divine self-consistency: YHWH cannot deny himself (2 Timothy 2:13). The I-will-not-give-my-glory-to-another is the monotheistic claim that the NT surprisingly applies to Jesus: the Father who sends the Son shares his glory with him — the glory you have given me I have given to them (John 17:22). The glory that YHWH refuses to share with idols he gives to the Son — because the Son is not another but is within YHWH's own identity.
12: Listen to me, O Jacob, and Israel, whom I called: I am he; I am the first; I am also the last. The third statement of the first-and-last title in Isaiah (also 41:4 and Isaiah 44:6) — here with the emphatic I am he (anî hû) preceding it. This triple occurrence establishes the title as a central identity marker. The Risen Christ applies it to himself three times in Revelation: I am the First and the Last (Revelation 1:17), I am the First and the Last, who died and came to life again (Revelation 2:8), I am the Alpha and the Omega, the First and the Last, the Beginning and the End (Revelation 22:13). No other OT title is more explicitly transferred from YHWH to the glorified Christ in the NT.
13: Indeed my own hand laid the foundations of the earth, and my right hand spread out the heavens; when I call to them, they stand up together. The Creator's right-hand act of laying earth's foundations and spreading out the heavens is applied to the Son in Hebrews 1:10-12 (citing Psalms 102:25-27: In the beginning, Lord, you laid the foundations of the earth, and the heavens are the work of your hands). The Psalm is applied to the Son — the one through whom YHWH made the universe (Hebrews 1:2). The right-hand Creator of Isaiah 48:13 is identified by the NT with the pre-incarnate Son who is the agent of creation (John 1:3, Colossians 1:16).
14: All of you, assemble and listen! Who among them has foretold these things? YHWH loves the one he has chosen — he will carry out his purpose against Babylon, and his arm will be against the Chaldeans. The one whom YHWH loves and has chosen — here likely Cyrus as his instrument, though the language overlaps with the Servant's election — will execute YHWH's purpose against Babylon. The loved-and-chosen pattern links Cyrus as political type to the Servant as the supreme loved-and-chosen. In the NT, the Father declares his love for the Son at both the baptism and transfiguration: this is my Son, whom I love (Matthew 3:17, Isaiah 17:5) — the beloved and chosen who carries out YHWH's ultimate purpose.
15: I, even I, have spoken and called him; I brought him, and he will succeed in his mission. The emphatic I-even-I (anî anî) — the same structure as 43:11 and Isaiah 43:25 — applied to the calling-and-sending of the chosen instrument. The success-in-mission guarantee is the divine sovereignty over the one sent: what YHWH sends, he brings to its purpose. In the NT: Jesus says of his own mission: I have completed the work you gave me to do (John 17:4); it is finished (John 19:30). The mission given by YHWH succeeds because YHWH is its ground and guarantor.
16: Come near to me and hear this: from the beginning I have not spoken in secret; from the time it came to be, I have been there. And now the Lord YHWH has sent me, and his Spirit. The dramatic shift to the speaker saying the Lord YHWH has sent me — a voice within the divine speech that is distinct from YHWH and yet speaks for him. This is the most explicit Servant/Spirit-sending in Isaiah before Isaiah 61: the Spirit-sent envoy of YHWH who can say the Lord YHWH has sent me. Jesus applies the parallel text in Isaiah 61:1-2 to himself at Nazareth (Luke 4:18): the Spirit of the Lord is on me, because he has anointed me to proclaim. The triune shape of v 16 — YHWH sends the envoy-with-the-Spirit — is the proto-Trinitarian mission structure that the NT makes explicit.
17: Thus says YHWH, your Redeemer, the Holy One of Israel: I am YHWH your God, who teaches you for your good, who directs you in the way you should go. The teaching-for-your-good is the description of the Torah's purpose — not burden but benefit. YHWH as teacher (Rabbi) is the role that Jesus explicitly takes in the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5-7) and in John's Gospel (John 13:13: you call me Teacher and Lord, and rightly so). The one who teaches-for-your-good is the Good Shepherd who leads in the right paths (Psalms 23:3) — the same YHWH who directs in the way-you-should-go now incarnate as the one who is the Way (John 14:6).
18: Oh, that you had paid attention to my commandments! Then your peace would have been like a river, and your righteousness like the waves of the sea. The divine lament — oh-that-you-had-listened — is the most tender verse in Isaiah 48. The peace-like-a-river and righteousness-like-the-sea are the full-and-overflowing images of shalom: not merely the absence of war but the presence of flourishing life at every level. Jesus cites this tradition when he weeps over Jerusalem: if you, even you, had only known on this day what would bring you peace — but now it is hidden from your eyes (Luke 19:42). The oh-that of Isaiah 48:18 becomes the weeping lament of Jesus over the city that did not hear.
19: Your offspring would have been like the sand and your descendants like its grains; their name would not have been cut off or destroyed from before me. The potential-that-was-forfeited: the Abrahamic promise of descendants like the sand of the sea (Genesis 22:17) could have been fully realized if Israel had listened. The cut-off name is the reversal of the eternal-name of Isaiah 56:5. In the NT: the promises to Abraham are fulfilled in Christ (Galatians 3:16 — the seed is one person, Christ) and in the community of faith (Galatians 3:29: if you belong to Christ, then you are Abraham's seed, heirs according to the promise). What was forfeited by natural Israel's disobedience is inherited by the Servant's community through faith.
20: Go out from Babylon, flee from the Chaldeans! Declare this with a shout of joy, proclaim it; send it out to the end of the earth; say, YHWH has redeemed his servant Jacob. The exodus-from-Babylon command is both historical (return from exile) and eschatological (the ultimate coming-out from the powers of this age). Paul cites the underlying theology in 2 Corinthians 6:17 (come out from them and be separate, says the Lord) and John's Apocalypse makes it explicit: Come out of her, my people, so that you will not share in her sins (Revelation 18:4). The exodus-from-Babylon is the NT's pattern of the Christian's separation from the worldly system that opposes YHWH's reign — the final-Exodus begun in the cross and completed at the parousia.
21: They did not thirst when he led them through the deserts; he made water flow for them from the rock; he split the rock and the water gushed out. The new Exodus recalls the original Exodus water-from-the-rock provision (Exodus 17:6): YHWH's past faithfulness in the wilderness guarantees his faithfulness in the new wilderness journey. Paul identifies the Rock of the wilderness with Christ: they all drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4). The water-from-the-rock is the living water that flows from the side of the crucified Jesus (John 19:34) — the fulfillment of the rock-struck-in-the-desert that accompanied Israel through the wilderness.
22: There is no peace, says YHWH, for the wicked. The refrain that closes both the first and second sections of the latter part of Isaiah (57:21 repeats it). The no-peace-for-the-wicked is the negative form of the gospel's shalom: the peace that passes understanding (Philippians 4:7) is available to those who come to YHWH through the Servant; those who reject the Servant's mediation remain in the unresolved state of v 18's tragedy. The peace-like-a-river that was available to obedient Israel (v 18) but forfeited by disobedience is the same peace that Jesus gives: my peace I give you, not as the world gives (John 14:27) — but only to those who receive the Servant-given shalom.
